So we're all aligned: we close the acquisition this quarter and fund it with debt. Felipe, you're happy with that?
I'm with you on the strategic logic, Diana. I genuinely am. But I'd gently push back on the timing. I have one reservation, and I think it's worth a minute.
Go on. Though I'll say the board is keen to move.

I take that point, and I'm not trying to slow us down for its own sake. But could we be underestimating what this debt does to our covenants? While I concede the target is right, I'd argue the timing might be a quarter too early.
That's a fair challenge. What would change your mind?

If we secured the refinancing first, I'd be fully behind it. That's my sticking point, not the deal, the sequence. On everything else we have common ground; on this one figure, I'd ask to stand my ground.
I'm with you on the logic, but I'd gently push back on the timing.
While I take your point, could we be underestimating the downside?
I have one reservation, and I think it's worth a minute.
On everything else we have common ground; on this one figure, I'd stand my ground.
I beg to differ, and I'll tell you exactly why.

I have chaired four boards, and I will tell you the single most dangerous sound in a boardroom. It is the sound of everyone agreeing. When a proposal goes round the table and every head nods, I do not feel reassured. I feel frightened, because it means one of two things: either we are all right, which is rare, or nobody in the room is willing to say what they actually think, which is common. So the first thing I do with a new finance chief is give them explicit permission to disagree with me, out loud, in front of the others. I tell them: your job is not to make me comfortable, it is to make me correct. The ones who take me up on it, who push back when the numbers demand it and concede gracefully when they turn out to be wrong, are the ones I trust with everything. The yes-people I watch very carefully, because a board of yes-people has no brakes.

I teach executives how to disagree without turning a meeting into a war, and the whole craft comes down to one thing: sequence. Most people lead with the disagreement. No, I do not think that works. And the moment the word no lands on the table, the other person stops listening and starts defending. So I teach the opposite order. Start with what is true in their position. Concede it honestly, out loud, so they know they have been heard. Then hedge what is genuinely uncertain: it might be, I am not sure, could we be. And only then, when the temperature in the room is low, do you name the one thing you cannot move on, and there you are completely unequivocal. Diplomatic disagreement is not weakness, and it is not softness. It is the ability to hold your ground on the thing that matters while giving way on the things that do not, and to leave the room with the relationship stronger, not weaker.
